text: The surgeon addresses adhesions or scar tissue between the sigmoid colon and rectum, likely due to intermittent intussusception, and begins the procedure by restoring normal anatomical alignment.
The surgeon has freed the redundant sigmoid colon, revealing a large lipoma occupying most of the colonic lumen. A tattoo is visible distal to the lipoma, marking its location.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the thin, floppy mesentery of the sigmoid colon, progressing towards the proximal resection margin and then moving downwards, facilitating a straightforward segmental resection.
The surgeon completes the dissection at the rectosigmoid junction, preparing to use the vessel sealer for further tissue manipulation.
The surgeon opens the proximal colon and carefully cleans off the surrounding fat to prepare the area for placing a purse string suture.
The surgeon uses a thoracic ring forcep to extract the specimen, with a visible lipoma extruding during the process. The specimen is removed smoothly without excessive force on the rectum.
The surgeon uses two points of retraction to position the anvil stationary, then pulls the lips of the colon over it. A purse string suture is applied to secure a cuff of tissue around the anvil.

The surgeon prepares the anvil and begins placing a purse string suture around the rectum, ensuring overlap on the right side for a secure closure. The suture is cinched tightly to accommodate a 33mm EEA stapler, aiming to deploy the spike through the center of the purse string.
The surgeon operates from below and at the console to align and connect the two sections of the colon, then returns between the patient's legs to fire the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the scope reaches up to and above the anastomosis site.
